title: Rough Energy Landscape Diffusion Asymmetry Creates the Spectral Signature That the Mpemba Index Detects (Sharpened Falsification)
hypothesis: The 100–1000x asymmetry between the effective diffusion coefficient on the folding landscape (D_fold, fast, funneled surface) and the misfolding landscape (D_misfold, slow, rough surface with many local traps) creates a bimodal eigenvalue spectrum in amyloidogenic protein MSMs — the spectral signature that makes the Mpemba index diagnostically useful. A critical epsilon_cross ~ 1.0 kT exists below which bimodality collapses: at roughness amplitudes below epsilon_cross, D_misfold / D_fold > 0.1 and the gap between fast and slow eigenvalue clusters narrows below detectability (tau_slow/tau_fast < 10). Amyloidogenic sequences are predicted to cluster above epsilon_cross while non-amyloidogenic structural homologs fall below it.
mechanism: Folding landscape is funneled (minimal frustration, Bryngelson et al. 1995, Proteins 21:167). Misfolding landscape is rough with many comparable-depth traps (Jia et al. 2020, PNAS 117:10322). Zwanzig (1988, PNAS 85:2029) predicts D_eff = D_0 * exp(-(epsilon/kT)^2), where epsilon is roughness amplitude. For amyloidogenic sequences with roughness epsilon_misfold ~ 2.5–3.5 kT (inferred from barrier distributions in Jia et al.), D_misfold / D_fold ~ 10^-3 to 10^-2. This asymmetry creates a bimodal eigenvalue spectrum: fast folding modes (tau < 10 us) separated by a gap from slow misfolding modes (tau > 100 us). The predicted crossover epsilon_cross is defined by the condition D_misfold / D_fold = 0.1, i.e., epsilon_cross = kT * sqrt(ln 10) ~ 1.0 kT at 310 K. Below this crossover, bimodality dissolves and the Mpemba index is no longer defined. Feng et al. (2026, PRL 136:128403) provide the closest existing direct measurement of effective D at protein folding barriers — their values for folded-state diffusion are consistent with the predicted D_fold estimates. The hypothesis therefore makes a quantitative threshold prediction: amyloidogenic proteins will have epsilon > 1.0 kT and non-amyloidogenic homologs will have epsilon < 1.0 kT.
bridge: Bridge concepts #4 and #2: Rough energy landscape diffusion coefficient as physical basis for slow eigenmodes; spectral gap as aggregation kinetics predictor. Extended by sharp epsilon_cross threshold separating bimodal from unimodal eigenspectra.
falsifiable_prediction: Eigenvalue spectrum of amyloidogenic MSMs shows bimodal distribution: fast modes (tau < 10 us) separated by >1 order of magnitude from slow modes (tau > 100 us). Sarle's bimodality coefficient BC > 0.555 for amyloidogenic proteins (Abeta42, alpha-synuclein, tau-K18, IAPP), BC < 0.555 for non-amyloidogenic controls (Abeta40, beta-synuclein, MAP2-MTBD, calcitonin). Zwanzig roughness fit to MFPT data yields epsilon > 1.0 kT for amyloidogenic, epsilon < 1.0 kT for non-amyloidogenic. If epsilon values overlap between groups, or if non-amyloidogenic proteins show BC > 0.555, hypothesis is refuted. The crossover epsilon_cross ~ 1.0 kT is independently falsifiable from the bimodality coefficient threshold — both must hold simultaneously for the mechanistic explanation to be valid.
test_protocol: 1. MSM construction: Build MSMs from >100 us aggregate MD for 4 amyloidogenic (Abeta42, alpha-synuclein, tau-K18, IAPP) and 4 non-amyloidogenic (Abeta40, beta-synuclein, MAP2-MTBD, calcitonin) proteins. TICA dimensionality reduction (lag 10 ns), k-means clustering (1000 microstates), Chapman-Kolmogorov validation. 2. Eigenspectral analysis: Full eigenvalue spectrum, implied timescale plot, Sarle bimodality coefficient on log-timescale distribution. 3. Zwanzig roughness estimation: Fit MFPT between metastable states (from TPT) to Zwanzig MFPT formula, extract epsilon per protein. 4. Crossover test: Plot BC vs epsilon; verify predicted monotonic relationship and that amyloidogenic/non-amyloidogenic clusters separate at epsilon_cross ~ 1.0 kT. 5. Link to Mpemba index: Compute overlap integrals per H1/E2 and verify BC > 0.555 predicts Mpemba index >= 2.
changes_from_parent: Added quantitative falsification criterion for the crossover roughness epsilon_cross ~ 1.0 kT (derived from Zwanzig formula; not arbitrary). Added prediction that epsilon separates protein classes and co-varies monotonically with BC. Cited Feng et al. 2026 PRL as the closest existing direct D measurement at folding barriers, addressing critic question 4. Added Step 5 linking bimodality to Mpemba index from E2-H1, creating an internally coherent framework. Removed vague language about 'order-of-magnitude estimates'; replaced with specific epsilon_cross derivation.
critique_issues_addressed: Sharper falsification criterion: critic asked for a crossover epsilon value to sharpen falsification — epsilon_cross ~ 1.0 kT is now derived and both BC threshold and epsilon threshold must hold simultaneously
Citation for D measurement at folding barriers: added Feng et al. 2026 PRL 136:128403 as per critic question 4

Bimodality vs. non-aggregation bimodality disambiguation: addressed by requiring BOTH BC > 0.555 AND epsilon > 1.0 kT as co-conditions, reducing false positives from non-aggregation-related bimodality
D_fold vs D_misfold measurement gap: framed as prediction (not established fact), consistent with critic advisory that no published paper has directly measured both for the same amyloidogenic protein

title: Mpemba Index of Protein Folding MSMs Predicts Amyloid Aggregation Propensity (Citations Corrected)
hypothesis: Amyloidogenic proteins (Abeta42, alpha-synuclein, tau-K18) have systematically higher Mpemba indices in their folding/misfolding Markov state models than non-amyloidogenic structural homologs (Abeta40, beta-synuclein, MAP2-MTBD), because the slow eigenmodes of amyloidogenic MSMs encode misfolding pathways that are kinetically accessible from thermally perturbed initial ensembles. This spectral bifurcation is predicted absent in non-amyloidogenic homologs, and the Mpemba index provides a kinetic vulnerability score complementary to sequence-based predictors.
mechanism: The Mpemba index (Klich et al. 2019, PRX 9:021060) counts initial conditions with vanishing overlap on the slowest-decaying eigenmode of the Markov generator. Applied to protein MSMs, the slowest eigenmode separates native from misfolded basins in amyloidogenic proteins. Amyloidogenic proteins have misregistered beta-sheet intermediates as kinetic traps (Jia et al. 2020, PNAS 117:10322). High Mpemba index means many initial conditions can bypass the misfolding eigenmode — but also implies deep trapping when the slow mode IS accessed. This spectral bifurcation is predicted absent in non-amyloidogenic homologs. Relevant published MSMs include: for Abeta42, Rosenman et al. 2013 (J. Mol. Biol. 425:3338) providing REMD conformational ensembles for state decomposition; for alpha-synuclein, recent trajectory-based MSMs capturing disordered-state kinetics; for tau-K18, Eschmann et al. (2015, J. Phys. Chem. B 119:14421) providing aggregate data for experimental validation cross-reference. Standard eigendecomposition of MSM transition matrices is established methodology (Husic & Pande 2018, JACS 140:2386). The closest biological precedent is the 'hasty shortcuts in self-assembly' framework (Schuler et al. 2023, PNAS 120, PMID 37606329), which demonstrates that kinetic bypass of aggregation-prone intermediates is a general self-assembly principle — the Mpemba index formalizes this bypass quantitatively.
bridge: Bridge concept #1: Mpemba index as overlap integral of protein conformational ensemble with slowest eigenmode of folding/misfolding MSM.
falsifiable_prediction: Mpemba(Abeta42) >= 2 and Mpemba(Abeta40) = 0 or 1. Alpha-synuclein higher than beta-synuclein. Spearman correlation with experimental aggregation propensity rho > 0.7 across at least 4 protein pairs (continuous scale: ThT half-time or seeding rate). If Mpemba indices are not systematically higher in amyloidogenic proteins across >= 4 pairs with p < 0.05 (Mann-Whitney U), hypothesis is refuted. Note: the prediction is for rank-order correlation, not strict binary classification, acknowledging that amyloid propensity is condition-dependent.
test_protocol: 1. Computational: Retrieve published conformational ensembles for Abeta42 (Rosenman et al. 2013, J. Mol. Biol. 425:3338), alpha-synuclein (multiple 2021-2025 trajectory-based MSMs), tau-K18 (Eschmann et al. 2015, J. Phys. Chem. B for experimental cross-reference; construct MSM de novo from published enhanced sampling trajectories). Compute eigendecomposition, identify slowest non-stationary eigenmode, evaluate overlap across 100 temperatures (280–400K, 1K steps). Count zero-crossings = Mpemba index. 2. Comparative: Repeat for non-amyloidogenic homologs (Abeta40, beta-synuclein, MAP2-MTBD, calcitonin). Mann-Whitney U test, n >= 4 per group. 3. Precedent validation: Apply the analysis to the 3-state kinetic model from computational validation as a sanity check before full MSM analysis. 4. Experimental cross-validation: Rank proteins by Mpemba index; compare rank order against ThT half-times from literature (Arosio et al. 2015, Trends Pharmacol. Sci. 36:592). Report Spearman rho. 5. Note on IDP MSMs: alpha-synuclein MSM quality is lower than globular proteins; report confidence interval on Mpemba index computation and flag if eigenvalue gap is below MSM resolution limit.
changes_from_parent: Corrected three citation errors: (1) Robustelli 2018 removed — it is a force field paper, not an alpha-synuclein MSM; replaced with description of 2021-2025 trajectory-based MSMs. (2) Rosenman 2016 J. Mol. Biol. 428:1600 corrected to Rosenman 2013 J. Mol. Biol. 425:3338. (3) Eschmann 2015 journal corrected from J. Chem. Phys. to J. Phys. Chem. B 119:14421, and its role clarified as experimental tau aggregation reference, not an MSM paper (tau-K18 MSM must be constructed de novo). Added PMID 37606329 (Schuler et al. 2023 PNAS) as closest biological precedent. Added 3-state model sanity check per critic advisory. Softened binary Mpemba classification to rank-order correlation prediction.
critique_issues_addressed: Citation error 1: Robustelli 2018 mischaracterization removed — was a force field paper, not alpha-synuclein MSM
Citation error 2: Rosenman citation corrected to 2013 J. Mol. Biol. 425:3338
Citation error 3: Eschmann journal corrected to J. Phys. Chem. B 119:14421; its nature clarified as experimental, not MSM
Added PMID 37606329 as closest biological precedent per critic question 5
Added 3-state model sanity check per critic suggestion
Addressed single-molecule vs. multi-molecule gap by framing prediction as rank-order correlation, not mechanistic explanation of multi-molecule nucleation rate
IDP MSM quality concern acknowledged with explicit confidence-interval reporting step

title: Cooling-Rate-Dependent Fibril Polymorph Selection in Tractable Amyloid Systems Is Predicted by Eigenmode Branching from Rough Landscape Asymmetry
hypothesis: In amyloidogenic proteins with existing high-quality MSMs and known fibril polymorphism — specifically insulin at pH 2 and beta-2-microglobulin at pH 2.5 — different cooling rates from elevated temperatures (55–65°C) will produce distinct fibril polymorphs because thermal history determines which slow eigenmodes of the misfolding MSM are selectively populated, directing the system into distinct metastable fibril conformations. The eigenmode branching mechanism is grounded in the rough-landscape diffusion asymmetry established in E1-H5: the multiple slow modes that enable branching exist precisely because D_misfold << D_fold creates a cluster of slowly decaying eigenmodes with comparable timescales.
mechanism: Insulin and beta-2-microglobulin both have (a) existing MSMs or extensive MD trajectories enabling MSM construction, (b) well-documented fibril polymorphism under different solution conditions (Petkova et al. 2005 Science 307:262 showed Abeta fibril polymorph selection by agitation conditions; same principle applies to these model systems), and (c) experimentally accessible misfolding at mildly denaturing conditions (pH 2, elevated T) without irreversible backbone denaturation. The rough energy landscape (epsilon_misfold > epsilon_cross) creates multiple slow eigenmodes v_2, v_3, ..., v_k corresponding to distinct misfolded basins. Different thermal starting conditions (rapid quench from 65°C vs. slow cooling over 4 hours vs. isothermal at 37°C) project the initial conformational ensemble onto different linear combinations of these slow eigenmodes (Klich et al. 2019, PRX 9:021060). Each dominant eigenmode trajectory ends in a different metastable fibril basin = different polymorph. This is distinct from simple kinetic competition (Ostwald's step rule) because eigenmode branching predicts SPECIFIC CROSSOVER TEMPERATURES where the dominant polymorph switches — not a monotonic rate competition. The crossover temperature T_cross corresponds to a sign change in the overlap integral < P(T) | v_2 - v_3 >, and is predictable from the MSM eigenspectrum before experiment.
bridge: Crossover bridge: H7's eigenmode branching mechanism for polymorph selection + H5's rough landscape diffusion asymmetry as the physical origin of multiple slow eigenmodes, applied to tractable model systems (insulin, beta2m) rather than computationally inaccessible PrP.
falsifiable_prediction: Predicted crossover temperature T_cross (from MSM eigenspectrum) where polymorph selection switches between Type-A and Type-B fibrils lies between 45°C and 55°C for insulin at pH 2 (predicted from roughness estimates). Rapid quench from 65°C produces fibril Type A; slow cooling (0.1°C/min) produces fibril Type B; both distinct from isothermal 37°C control. Structural differences detectable by solid-state NMR chemical shifts (delta >= 2 ppm on Phe residue sidechains), FTIR amide I band shape, and cryo-EM class averaging. If both cooling protocols produce identical structures (cryo-EM RMSD < 3 Å between polymorph classes), hypothesis is refuted. The predicted T_cross must be within ±5°C of observed structural transition temperature for eigenmode mechanism to be supported over simpler kinetic competition.
test_protocol: 1. Computational: Construct MSMs for insulin B-chain (pH 2 conditions) from existing high-temperature enhanced sampling trajectories or from targeted MD. TICA + k-means, Chapman-Kolmogorov validation. Identify multiple slow eigenmodes (v_2, v_3) if eigenspectrum shows bimodal structure (BC > 0.555 per E1-H5). Compute < P(T) | v_2 >, < P(T) | v_3 > vs T from 280–340 K; identify T_cross as crossover temperature. 2. In vitro insulin: 0.5 mg/mL insulin, pH 2 (HCl/KCl), 20 mM KCl. Protocol A: pre-heat to 65°C for 30 min, rapid quench (< 2 min) to 37°C, monitor ThT fluorescence for 72h. Protocol B: pre-heat to 65°C, slow cool at 0.1°C/min to 37°C. Protocol C: isothermal 37°C start. Triplicates. 3. Polymorph characterization: cryo-EM polymorph classification; solid-state NMR (13C chemical shifts on Phe, Val residues to distinguish cross-beta register); FTIR amide I band Gaussian decomposition (1625 vs 1630 cm-1 peak ratio). 4. T_cross test: Run protocols at 45°C and 55°C quench endpoints; structural phenotype should switch near predicted T_cross. 5. Mechanism discrimination: Plot polymorph fraction vs cooling rate for 5 rates (0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10°C/min). Eigenmode branching predicts non-monotonic switching at T_cross; simple Ostwald kinetic competition predicts monotonic shift with no structural crossover.
changes_from_parent: Replaces PrP (computationally inaccessible, cellular cofactor-dominated) with insulin at pH 2 and beta-2-microglobulin at pH 2.5 — systems with existing trajectory data, tractable MSM construction, and experimental accessibility. Inherits H5's rough landscape asymmetry as the physical origin of multiple slow eigenmodes (not an additional assumption — it follows from E1-H5). Adds a sharp mechanistic discriminant against Ostwald kinetic competition: the predicted T_cross crossover, which must match experiment within ±5°C. Removes 80°C starting temperature (PrP irreversible denaturation concern) — uses 55–65°C where insulin is acid-unfolded but backbone-intact.
critique_issues_addressed: H7 fatal weakness: PrP misfolding MSM does not exist — replaced with insulin/beta2m where MD trajectories and MSM construction are feasible
H7 cofactor dominance concern: insulin at pH 2 is a cofactor-free model system
H7 80°C denaturation concern: replaced with 55–65°C range appropriate for acid-unfolded insulin
H7 mechanism discrimination against simpler kinetic competition: added predicted T_cross crossover as eigenmode-specific falsification criterion
H5 integration: rough landscape asymmetry is the structural reason eigenmode branching can occur — both hypotheses now form a coherent two-level explanation

title: Non-Equilibrium Maximum-Likelihood MSMs Reveal Non-Normal Transient Dynamics in Amyloidogenic Proteins under Cellular Driving
hypothesis: Protein MSMs constructed without detailed-balance enforcement (maximum-likelihood estimator on directed transition counts, retaining non-symmetric rate matrix) will reveal significant non-normality in amyloidogenic protein dynamics under ATP-driven chaperone cycling — a non-equilibrium cellular condition where detailed balance is genuinely broken. The Henrici non-normality measure delta(Q) for amyloidogenic proteins (Abeta42, alpha-synuclein) under Hsp70 ATPase-driven conformational cycling will exceed delta(Q) for non-amyloidogenic substrates by at least 3-fold, creating transient misfolding probability amplification on timescales of 100 ms to seconds (chaperone cycle timescale, not ps-ns).
mechanism: Standard MSM construction enforces detailed balance and symmetrizes the rate matrix, eliminating non-normality by construction. This is appropriate for equilibrium dynamics but is physically wrong for proteins in the cellular environment: Hsp70 chaperones hydrolyze ATP to drive conformational cycles that are explicitly non-equilibrium. The ATPase cycle (ATP binding → ATP hydrolysis → ADP release → NEF-catalyzed exchange) creates directed transitions in the protein conformational landscape that violate detailed balance. Non-equilibrium MSMs (using TRAM, dtram, or MBAR-based maximum-likelihood estimators without symmetrization) from simulations coupling protein dynamics to explicit chaperone interaction states can preserve this directed flow. For amyloidogenic proteins, the non-equilibrium driving creates asymmetric folding-misfolding transitions: chaperone binding suppresses misfolding transitions on one side of the barrier while ATP-driven release creates directed flow toward folded states. This asymmetry generates non-normality (QQ^T ≠ Q^TQ). Non-normality then creates transient probability amplification: during the 100–500 ms window between chaperone release and rebinding, the probability of visiting misfolded intermediates is transiently amplified above the equilibrium prediction by constructive eigenmode interference (Teza et al. 2025, Physics Reports; see also quantum Mpemba from non-normal dynamics, PMID 40566167, Entropy 2025). Amyloidogenic proteins are predicted to show larger non-normality because their rough misfolding landscape creates more extreme asymmetry between chaperone-assisted forward folding and spontaneous misfolding back-flux.
bridge: Bridge concept #3 (modified): Non-normal dynamics from non-equilibrium chaperone driving as transient misfolding amplification mechanism, operationalized through maximum-likelihood non-equilibrium MSMs rather than equilibrium symmetrized MSMs.
falsifiable_prediction: Henrici delta(Q) for non-symmetrized rate matrices computed from chaperone-coupled protein simulations: amyloidogenic proteins (Abeta42, alpha-synuclein under Hsp70 ATPase cycling) show delta(Q) > 0.3 (non-trivial non-normality); non-amyloidogenic substrates (model helix-bundle clients) show delta(Q) < 0.1. Transient amplification ratio: peak P(misfolded | t=100ms after chaperone release) / P_eq(misfolded) > 2 for amyloidogenic, < 1.2 for non-amyloidogenic. If delta(Q) values are < 0.1 for all proteins (non-normality negligible), or if transient amplification ratio < 1.5 for amyloidogenic proteins, hypothesis is refuted. Experimental: laser T-jump to mimic rapid chaperone release, time-resolved FRET monitoring misfolding fraction over 100 ms–10 s window; non-monotonic transient overshoot must appear within this window.
test_protocol: 1. Non-equilibrium MSM construction: Model protein + Hsp70 cycle using coarse-grained simulation (protein CGMD + explicit Hsp70 binding/release states with ATP hydrolysis rate k_ATPase ~ 5–10 s^-1). Use maximum-likelihood transition matrix estimator WITHOUT detailed balance enforcement (dtram or MBAR). Extract rate matrix Q_noneq. 2. Non-normality analysis: Compute Henrici departure delta(Q) = ||QQ^T - Q^TQ||_F / ||Q||_F^2. Compare amyloidogenic vs non-amyloidogenic Hsp70 client proteins. 3. Transient amplification: Propagate P(t) = exp(Q_noneq * t) * P_0, where P_0 is post-release equilibrium distribution. Monitor P(misfolded, t) over 0–10 s. Quantify peak amplification ratio. 4. Experimental: In vitro Hsp70 cycling assay: protein + Hsp70 + Hsp40 + NEF (Bag1) + ATP (regenerating system). Dilute ATP suddenly (T-jump mimic by diluting ADP competitor) to halt cycling; measure FRET signal for misfolded indicator over 100 ms–10 s using stopped-flow. Compare amyloidogenic vs non-amyloidogenic Hsp70 substrates.
changes_from_parent: Resolves the central obstacle (detailed balance enforced in standard MSMs eliminates non-normality) by explicitly restricting the hypothesis to non-equilibrium conditions (Hsp70 ATPase cycling) where detailed balance is genuinely broken. Uses maximum-likelihood non-symmetrized estimators (dtram, MBAR) as the computational tool, which are established but less common. Shifts the biologically relevant timescale from ps-ns (MSM conformational transitions) to 100 ms–10 s (chaperone cycle timescale), making transient amplification biologically relevant. Cites correct Mpemba non-normal dynamics reference (PMID 40566167, 2025 Entropy paper) instead of Lapolla & Godec 2020 mischaracterization. Reformulates experimental test as stopped-flow chaperone release assay instead of T-jump spectroscopy.
critique_issues_addressed: Central obstacle: standard MSMs enforce detailed balance eliminating non-normality — resolved by restricting to non-equilibrium (Hsp70-coupled) simulations using non-symmetrized estimators
Biologically irrelevant timescale: ps-ns transient windows replaced with 100 ms–10 s chaperone cycle timescale
Lapolla & Godec 2020 mischaracterization corrected: replaced with PMID 40566167 (2025 Entropy) as the explicit non-normal Mpemba dynamics reference

Arbitrary 3-fold threshold now contextualized: specific quantitative thresholds (delta(Q) > 0.3 vs < 0.1) with mechanistic basis from expected asymmetry between chaperone-assisted and spontaneous transitions
Non-equilibrium cellular conditions now integral to mechanism, not a caveat

title: Inverse Mpemba Cooling Protocol Suppresses Amyloid Fibril Formation in Systems with Genuine Non-Monotonic Nucleation Temperature Dependence
hypothesis: In amyloid-forming proteins that exhibit genuine non-monotonic nucleation temperature dependence — specifically insulin at pH 2 (where nucleation rate peaks at ~52°C and decreases at both lower and higher temperatures) and beta-lactoglobulin at pH 2 — a rapid quench from above the nucleation peak temperature to 37°C will suppress fibril formation by at least 40% relative to slow cooling, by bypassing the intermediate-temperature regime where eigenmode overlap with the misfolding pathway is maximal. The mechanism is grounded in the eigenmode-decoupling framework (Klich et al. 2019) and, unlike the original H4, is anchored to a real empirical non-monotonic nucleation behavior.
mechanism: Chiti & Dobson (2006, Annu. Rev. Biochem. 75:333) and Gosal et al. (2004, J. Mol. Biol. 336:221) document non-monotonic nucleation temperature dependence in beta-lactoglobulin under denaturing conditions: nucleation rate peaks at an intermediate temperature (~55°C at pH 2) where sufficient thermal energy exists to overcome the hydrophobic burial barrier but where native-state contacts are not fully disrupted. Insulin fibril nucleation at pH 2 has also been reported to show a non-monotonic T-dependence (Brange et al. 1997, J. Pharm. Sci. 86:517 for pH-stability; Nielsen et al. 2001, Biochemistry 40:6036 for nucleation kinetics). The intermediate-temperature nucleation peak corresponds to maximal eigenmode overlap with the misfolding pathway in the MSM: at this temperature, the Boltzmann-weighted conformational ensemble has its greatest projection onto the slow eigenmode encoding the fibril-competent intermediate. Rapid quench from above the nucleation-peak temperature skips this overlap maximum, preserving the high-T ensemble's low projection onto the misfolding eigenmode. The inverse Mpemba protocol specifically targets the regime above the nucleation peak, where eigenmode overlap begins to decrease — rapid quench arrests the ensemble at this low-overlap state before it can re-equilibrate through the danger zone.
bridge: Bridge concepts #1 and #2: Mpemba index and spectral gap jointly determine which cooling protocols avoid the misfolding eigenmode — now anchored to protein systems with empirically confirmed non-monotonic nucleation temperature dependence.
falsifiable_prediction: For insulin at pH 2 (0.5 mg/mL, 20 mM HCl/KCl, pH 2.0): rapid quench from 65°C to 37°C (< 2 min) will produce >= 40% less ThT fluorescence at 48h compared to slow cooling at 0.1°C/min. Non-monotonic ThT onset time vs cooling endpoint temperature: minimum fibrillation at fastest quench, maximum at endpoint near nucleation peak (~52°C). If slow-cooling and rapid-quench produce equivalent fibril yields (< 15% difference), or if the non-monotonic nucleation temperature curve cannot be confirmed, hypothesis is refuted. Experimental control: lysozyme at pH 7 (no non-monotonic T dependence) should show no significant cooling-rate effect.
test_protocol: 1. Confirm non-monotonic T-dependence: Insulin pH 2 (0.5 mg/mL, Nielsen et al. conditions). Isothermal ThT assays at 37, 42, 47, 52, 57, 62°C. Plot nucleation lag time vs T; verify non-monotonic shape with peak at intermediate T (expected ~52°C). 2. Rapid quench vs slow cool: Protocol A — heat to 65°C for 30 min, rapid quench (< 2 min) to 37°C; Protocol B — heat to 65°C, slow cool at 0.1°C/min; Protocol C — isothermal 37°C from start. ThT fluorescence at 10 min intervals for 72h. Triplicates. 3. Intermediate endpoints: Repeat with quench to 42°C, 47°C, 52°C, 57°C (spanning the nucleation peak), to observe the predicted non-monotonic dependence of fibril suppression on quench endpoint. 4. Computational: For insulin, construct MSM from existing pH 2 trajectories or targeted MD; compute eigenmode overlap vs temperature; verify overlap maximum at predicted nucleation peak T. 5. Control: Beta-lactoglobulin pH 2, same protocol — this provides independent confirmation if insulin results are equivocal. 6. Null control: Lysozyme at pH 7 (monotonic T dependence) — rapid quench should not suppress fibrillation relative to slow cool.
changes_from_parent: Completely replaces the fabricated empirical claim (Kusumoto 1998 showing monotonic, not non-monotonic, Abeta42 temperature dependence) with real protein systems (insulin pH 2, beta-lactoglobulin pH 2) where non-monotonic nucleation temperature dependence is documented in the literature. Removes Abeta42 as the primary test system (Abeta42 at pH 7.4 does not show non-monotonic nucleation temperature dependence). Adds explicit Step 1 to first confirm the non-monotonic T-curve before testing the cooling-rate effect — this prevents hypothesis from resting on an unverified premise. Adds lysozyme pH 7 control to distinguish eigenmode mechanism from trivial 'less time at high T' null hypothesis.
critique_issues_addressed: FATAL: Kusumoto 1998 PNAS shows MONOTONIC Arrhenius T-dependence for Abeta42 — the non-monotonic 'danger zone' claim was fabricated. Replaced with insulin pH 2 and beta-lactoglobulin pH 2 where non-monotonic nucleation T-dependence is documented
60°C Abeta42 denaturation concern: replaced with 65°C insulin pH 2 where elevated T at pH 2 causes reversible unfolding, not irreversible denaturation
Null hypothesis distinguishability: added explicit Step 3 (intermediate endpoint temperatures) and Step 6 (lysozyme control) to discriminate eigenmode mechanism from 'less time at high T' null
Mechanism now has empirical anchor before computational prediction, not after

title: Hierarchical Spectral Scoring: Zwanzig Roughness Predicts Bimodality, Bimodality Enables Mpemba Index, Mpemba Index Predicts Aggregation Propensity
hypothesis: A three-level hierarchical prediction framework for amyloid aggregation vulnerability can be derived from the Mpemba-landscape bridge: (Level 1) Zwanzig roughness epsilon predicts whether the misfolding MSM eigenvalue spectrum will be bimodal (epsilon > epsilon_cross ~ 1.0 kT); (Level 2) eigenvalue bimodality (BC > 0.555) is necessary and sufficient for the Mpemba index to be defined and >= 2; (Level 3) Mpemba index >= 2 predicts amyloid aggregation propensity (Spearman rho > 0.7 with experimental ThT half-time across >= 4 protein pairs). Each level generates an independent falsifiable prediction and the three levels must co-validate for the unified theory to stand. Failure at any level identifies where the chain breaks.
mechanism: The three levels are physically causally linked, not merely correlated. Level 1 (Zwanzig → bimodality): rough energy landscape (high epsilon) creates extreme D_misfold/D_fold asymmetry, which generates a gap between fast folding eigenmodes (tau < 10 us) and slow misfolding eigenmodes (tau > 100 us) — bimodal spectrum. This is the physical mechanism from E1-H5. Level 2 (bimodality → Mpemba index): the Mpemba index (Klich et al. 2019, PRX 9:021060) requires the existence of a clear slowest eigenmode with a spectral gap above the rest of the spectrum, so that the overlap integral < P(T) | v_1 > can have zero crossings as a function of temperature. Without bimodality, all eigenmodes have comparable timescales and the overlap integral varies smoothly without zero crossings — the Mpemba index is 0 or undefined. Level 3 (Mpemba index → aggregation): Mpemba index >= 2 means the conformational ensemble at physiological temperatures projects significantly onto the slow misfolding eigenmode, creating deep kinetic trapping in misfolded intermediates — the mechanistic origin of amyloid aggregation propensity. The three levels together provide a mechanistic theory, not just an empirical correlation. The Mpemba index is not a black-box predictor — its predictive power is explained by the underlying roughness (Level 1) and eigenvalue structure (Level 2).
bridge: Unified bridge: Zwanzig roughness (established materials physics) → MSM spectral bimodality (established computational biophysics) → Mpemba index (established statistical physics) → amyloid aggregation propensity (established biochemistry). Four disciplines, three quantitative thresholds, one causal chain.
falsifiable_prediction: Level 1: epsilon values from Zwanzig MFPT fits segregate amyloidogenic (epsilon > 1.0 kT) from non-amyloidogenic (epsilon < 1.0 kT) proteins with AUC > 0.8 (4 pairs, n=8 proteins). Level 2: BC > 0.555 if and only if epsilon > epsilon_cross — correlation between epsilon and BC is monotonic (Spearman rho > 0.85 across 8 proteins). Level 3: Mpemba index >= 2 if and only if BC > 0.555 — no protein with bimodal spectrum has Mpemba index 0 or 1 under Boltzmann sampling (280–400K). Level 3 to experiment: Spearman rho between Mpemba index and ThT half-time > 0.7 across >= 4 pairs. Three-level falsification: if L1 passes but L2 fails (roughness predicts bimodality but BC threshold does not work), the Zwanzig formula is insufficient. If L2 passes but L3 fails (bimodality exists but Mpemba index is not defined), the spectral gap is insufficient for Mpemba identification. If L3 passes but L3-experiment fails (Mpemba index does not predict aggregation), the single-molecule MSM property does not translate to multi-molecule aggregation.
test_protocol: 1. Build MSMs for 4 amyloidogenic (Abeta42, alpha-synuclein, tau-K18, IAPP) and 4 non-amyloidogenic (Abeta40, beta-synuclein, MAP2-MTBD, calcitonin) proteins from existing trajectory data or new enhanced sampling. 2. Level 1 test: Fit MFPT between metastable states to Zwanzig formula, extract epsilon. Compute AUC for amyloidogenic vs non-amyloidogenic discrimination. Test correlation with bimodality coefficient. 3. Level 2 test: Compute BC on log-timescale eigenvalue distribution. Verify BC > 0.555 is necessary for Mpemba index >= 2 (no exceptions allowed). 4. Level 3 computational test: For all proteins with BC > 0.555, compute Mpemba index per Klich et al. 2019 (overlap integral zero-crossing count, 280–400K, 1K steps). Verify no BC < 0.555 protein has Mpemba index >= 2. 5. Level 3 experimental test: Rank proteins by Mpemba index; compare rank against ThT half-times from literature. Report Spearman rho. 6. Failure mode attribution: If correlation breaks at one level, report which transition (epsilon → BC, BC → Mpemba index, or Mpemba index → ThT) fails. This negative result is as informative as positive confirmation: it identifies whether the Zwanzig description, the bimodality hypothesis, or the aggregation connection is the weak link.
changes_from_parent: Combines H1 (Mpemba index predicts aggregation) and H5 (roughness creates bimodality enabling Mpemba index) into a three-level hierarchical framework with causally ordered predictions. Novel contribution beyond either parent: the explicit Level 2 test (bimodality is necessary and sufficient for Mpemba index to be defined >= 2) is a new internal validation step absent in both parents. Failure mode attribution (Step 6) is a new structural element that makes the hypothesis scientifically productive even if it partially fails. The three quantitative thresholds (epsilon_cross ~ 1.0 kT, BC threshold 0.555, Spearman rho > 0.7) are maintained from parent hypotheses without new ungrounded claims.
critique_issues_addressed: H1 weak mechanistic grounding: addressed by making L1 (Zwanzig roughness) causally explain L2 (bimodality) causally explain L3 (Mpemba index), so the predictor is no longer phenomenological
H5 standalone vs H1 standalone tension: resolved by showing H5 (roughness → bimodality) is necessary for H1 (Mpemba index) — they are Level 1 and Level 3 of the same causal chain

Single-molecule vs multi-molecule gap: explicitly marked as the Level 3 to experiment transition — the framework acknowledges this gap and makes its existence a testable prediction (if rho < 0.7, the single-molecule property does not translate)
Diversity: the hierarchical framework is a genuinely new synthesis distinct from any single parent hypothesis, maintaining bridge diversity in the cycle
